name: Northern Irish Ulster Fry

ingredients.note: boiled and sliced, standing in for potato farl

steps: Fry the bacon until crisp, then set aside.
Fry the eggs in half the butter.
Warm the tomatoes in a small pan.
Fry the sliced potato in the remaining butter until golden, and plate everything together.

ingredients_full.note: boiled and sliced, standing in for potato farl

steps_detailed: Fry the bacon over medium heat until crisp, 3-4 minutes per side, then set aside.
Fry the eggs in half the butter, seasoning with black pepper.
Warm the tomatoes through in a small pan, 4-5 minutes.
Fry the sliced, pre-boiled potato in the remaining butter until golden and crisp on both sides, standing in for a proper potato farl.
A full Ulster fry traditionally centers on soda bread and potato farls rather than the toast an English breakfast leans on, with bacon just as central to both.

name: Northern Irish Soda Bread
steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, and a pinch of salt with the milk into a soft dough.
Shape into a round, cut a cross on top, and bake at 400F for 20-25 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, a pinch of salt, the melted butter, and the milk (soured briefly with the lime juice, standing in for buttermilk) into a soft, shaggy dough.
Shape into a round on a floured surface, cut a deep cross on top to help it cook through, and bake at 400F (200C) for 22-28 minutes until golden and it sounds hollow when tapped.
Soda bread rises from baking soda reacting with soured milk rather than yeast, which is why it comes together so quickly.

name: Northern Irish Porridge with Honey
steps: Simmer the oats in the milk for 6-8 minutes, stirring, until thick.
Top with the honey.
steps_detailed: Simmer the oats in the milk and cinnamon over medium-low heat, stirring often, 8-10 minutes, until thick and creamy.
Top generously with the honey before serving.

name: Northern Irish Champ

ingredients.note: finely chopped, standing in for scallion

steps: Simmer the onion in the milk over low heat, 4-5 minutes, to soften and mellow its bite.
Mash the potato thoroughly with the milk-onion mixture, half the butter, and pepper until smooth.
Spoon into a bowl, make a well in the center, and let the remaining butter melt into it - champ is traditionally eaten by dragging each forkful of potato through the pool of melted butter.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Onions, spring or scallions (includes tops and bulb), raw
ingredients_full.note: finely chopped, standing in for scallion

steps_detailed: Simmer the onion in the milk over low heat, 4-5 minutes, to soften and mellow its bite.
Mash the potato thoroughly with the milk-onion mixture, half the butter, and pepper until smooth.
Spoon into a bowl, make a well in the center, and let the remaining butter melt into it - champ is traditionally eaten by dragging each forkful of potato through the pool of melted butter.
Real scallions are used here now, giving the sharper, more specific flavor the dish actually calls for rather than yellow onion's milder substitute.

name: Northern Irish Colcannon
steps: Soften the cabbage in half the butter, 8-10 minutes.
Mash the potato with the milk and remaining butter, then fold in the cabbage.
steps_detailed: Soften the cabbage in half the butter over medium-low heat, 10-12 minutes, until fully tender.
Mash the potato with the milk, remaining butter, and pepper until smooth, then fold the cabbage through.
Colcannon differs from champ mainly by trading the onion for cabbage or kale, and is especially associated with Halloween in Northern Ireland, sometimes with small charms hidden inside.

name: Northern Irish Boxty
steps: Mix the grated and mashed potato with the flour and egg into a thick batter.
Fry spoonfuls in the butter, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the grated and mashed potato with the flour, egg, and pepper into a thick, well-combined batter.
Fry spoonfuls in the butter over medium heat, 4-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and cooked through.
Boxty uses both raw grated and cooked mashed potato in the same batter, giving it a texture distinct from a plain potato pancake.

name: Northern Irish Cheese and Potato Bake
steps: Layer the potato slices with the milk in a small dish and bake at 375F, covered, for 30 minutes.
Uncover, top with the cheese, and bake 10-15 more minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Layer the potato slices with the milk, butter, and pepper in a small baking dish, cover tightly, and bake at 375F (190C) for 32-35 minutes until the potato is tender.
Uncover, top generously with the cheese, and bake 12-15 more minutes until golden and bubbling.

name: Northern Irish Beef and Cabbage Stew
steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the potato, cabbage, and 1.5 cups water, and simmer 25-28 minutes until tender.
steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion and pepper over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the potato, cabbage, and 1.75 cups water, and simmer 28-32 minutes until all the vegetables are fully tender.

name: Northern Irish Lamb and Potato Pie
steps: Brown the lamb with the potato.
Make a simple pastry from the flour, butter, and cold water, line a small dish, fill with the lamb mixture, top with more pastry, and bake at 400F for 20-25 minutes.
steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with the potato and onion over medium-high heat, 10-12 minutes.
Rub the butter into the flour with a pinch of salt and enough cold water to form a firm pastry.
Line a small dish with half the pastry, fill with the lamb mixture, top with the remaining pastry, seal, and bake at 400F (200C) for 22-26 minutes until golden.

name: Northern Irish Baked Herring with Potato
steps: Layer the potato slices in a dish with half the butter and bake at 400F for 20 minutes.
Lay the herring on top, dot with the remaining butter, and bake 12-15 more minutes until cooked through. Finish with the lime juice.
steps_detailed: Layer the potato slices in a baking dish with half the butter and bake at 400F (200C) for 20-22 minutes until nearly tender.
Season the herring with pepper, lay it over the potato, dot with the remaining butter, and bake 14-16 more minutes until the fish flakes easily.
Finish with the lime juice just before serving.

name: Northern Irish Champ-Topped Shepherd's Pie

ingredients.note: finely chopped, mixed into the topping

steps: Brown the lamb.
Mix the mashed potato with half the onion and the butter for a champ-style topping, spread over the lamb, and bake at 400F for 15-20 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: finely chopped, mixed into the topping

steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with half the onion and the pepper over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Mash the potato with the butter and the remaining onion for a champ-style topping, spread over the lamb, and bake at 400F (200C) for 18-22 minutes until golden.
Folding scallion-style onion into the mash before topping the pie is a distinctly Northern Irish crossover between champ and shepherd's pie.

name: Northern Irish Potato Farls
steps: Mix the mashed potato with the flour and melted butter into a firm dough.
Pat into a round, cut into quarters, and cook on a dry griddle 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mix the mashed potato with the flour, melted butter, and pepper into a firm, workable dough.
Pat into a round about half an inch thick, cut into quarters (the traditional farl shape), and cook on a dry, hot griddle, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden with dark speckling.
Farls are a fixture of the Ulster fry, though they're just as often eaten on their own with butter.

name: Northern Irish Barmbrack
steps: Soak the currants in warm water for 20 minutes, then drain.
Mix the flour, baking powder, honey, and currants with egg and milk into a thick batter, and bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes.
steps_detailed: Soak the currants in warm water for 25-30 minutes to plump them, then drain well.
Mix the flour, baking powder, cinnamon, honey, egg, and drained currants with a splash of milk into a thick batter.

Pour into a small lined pan and bake at 350F (175C) for 35-40 minutes until a skewer comes out clean - barmbrack is traditionally baked with a hidden ring or coin inside for Halloween fortune-telling.

name: Northern Irish Cheese and Soda Bread Snack

ingredients.note: for a quick soda bread base

steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, and a pinch of salt with milk into a soft dough, shape into a small round, and bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes.
Split while warm and add the cheese.

ingredients_full.note: for a quick soda bread base

steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, a pinch of salt, and the milk into a soft dough, shape into a small round, and bake at 400F (200C) for 16-20 minutes until golden.
Split while still warm, butter generously, and add the cheese so it just begins to soften.

name: Northern Irish Herring and Cabbage Spread
steps: Mash the kippered herring with the butter and minced cabbage until well combined.
Serve with bread.
steps_detailed: Mash the kippered herring with the butter, minced cabbage, and lime juice until well combined and spreadable.
Serve with bread or farls - the cabbage adds a bit of crunch and freshness against the rich, smoky fish.

name: Northern Irish Rice Pudding with Honey
steps: Simmer the rice in the milk with the honey and cinnamon for 25-30 minutes until thick and creamy.
steps_detailed: Simmer the rice in the milk with the honey, cinnamon, and butter over low heat, stirring often, 28-32 minutes, until thick and creamy.
Serve warm, with an extra drizzle of honey if you like.

name: Northern Irish Currant Squares
steps: Cream the butter and sugar, mix in the flour and currants, press into a small pan, and bake at 350F for 20-22 minutes.
Cool and cut into squares.
steps_detailed: Cream the butter and sugar until light, then beat in the egg.
Fold in the flour, baking powder, and currants, press into a small lined pan, and bake at 350F (175C) for 22-26 minutes until golden and set.
Cool completely before cutting into squares - a simple, dense, currant-flecked traybake common at Northern Irish tea tables.

name: Northern Irish Milk and Honey Pudding
steps: Whisk the cornstarch into a little of the cold milk.
Warm the rest of the milk with the honey, whisk in the egg and cornstarch slurry, and cook gently, stirring, until thickened.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cornstarch into a little of the cold milk until smooth.
Warm the rest of the milk with the honey and cinnamon, whisk in the egg and cornstarch slurry, and cook over low heat, stirring constantly, 6-8 minutes until thickened to a soft custard.
Chill briefly or serve warm - a plain, gentle pudding built from little more than the dairy at hand.

name: Northern Irish Onion and Milk Sauce
steps: Soften the onion in the butter, 8-10 minutes.
Add the milk and simmer 5-6 minutes into a light sauce.
steps_detailed: Soften the onion in the butter over medium-low heat, 10-12 minutes, until fully tender and just turning golden.
Add the milk and pepper and simmer 6-8 minutes into a light, mildly sweet sauce - good spooned over champ, colcannon, or farls.

name: Northern Irish Honey Butter
steps: Beat the butter and honey together until smooth and light.
steps_detailed: Beat the butter, honey, and cinnamon together until smooth, light, and well combined.
Spread over warm soda bread or farls - simple, and better than either ingredient on its own.
